name: Oya Storm
description: Oya, also known as Iya Mesan, is a powerful and fierce Orisha (deity) in Yoruba religion. She is the Yoruba goddess of winds, storms, lightning, and violent change. Oya is often associated with the Niger River, which is called Odo Ọya in Yoruba. She is a warrior spirit, a protector of women, and a powerful force for transformation. Oya is known for her ability to bring about rapid change and destruction, clearing the way for new beginnings. She is a complex figure, embodying both fierce power and nurturing compassion. Oya is also strongly associated with death and rebirth, guiding souls to the afterlife and protecting the living from malevolent spirits. As a fierce warrior, she is often depicted wielding a sword or whip. Her colors are typically maroon, purple, and copper. Oya's power is often invoked in times of great upheaval or when seeking to overcome obstacles. She embodies the strength and resilience needed to navigate life's challenges and embrace change. She is also a patron of divination and psychic abilities. Oya is a vital figure in Yoruba spirituality, representing the dynamic and transformative forces of nature and the human spirit.
subtitle: Goddess of Winds, Storms, and Change

keyMyths.title: Oya and Shango
keyMyths.description: Oya was the favorite wife of Shango, the Orisha of thunder. She fought alongside him in battle and shared his power over lightning.

keyMyths.title: Oya's Transformation
keyMyths.description: Oya is known for her ability to transform into a buffalo. This represents her fierce power and connection to the animal kingdom.

keyMyths.title: Oya as a Guide
keyMyths.description: Oya guides the souls of the dead to the afterlife, helping them transition to the realm of the ancestors. She protects them from harm during this journey.

sources.author: Idowu, E. Bolaji
sources.description: A comprehensive study of Yoruba religious beliefs and practices.
